taskDescription: Drag the correct neuroanatomical terms into the blanks to complete statements about the pons.
textField:
The pons is divided into a ventral part called the *basis pontis* and a dorsal part called the *tegmentum*. The basis pontis contains transverse pontine fibres and scattered pontine nuclei that relay cortical input to the cerebellum.

The *facial* nerve fibres loop over the *abducens* nucleus to form the genu of CN VII, creating the facial *colliculus* on the 4th ventricle floor. This anatomical relationship explains why pontine lesions often affect both CN VI and CN VII together.

In locked-in syndrome caused by pontine haemorrhage, the patient is fully *conscious* but can only communicate through *vertical eye* movements. The corticospinal and corticobulbar tracts in the ventral pons are destroyed, but the reticular activating system in the tegmentum is spared.

The *middle cerebellar peduncle* is the largest peduncle and carries *pontocerebellar* fibres from the pontine nuclei to the cerebellum. The *trigeminal* nerve (CN V) emerges from the lateral aspect of the pons. The *locus coeruleus*, a pigmented nucleus in the pons, is the main source of noradrenergic projections to the cerebral cortex.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the anatomy of the pons.
